Importing Visual C++ 6.0 Projects

For the most part, the Xbox Development Kit for Visual Studio .NET automatically converts Xbox projects from Visual C++ 6.0 to Visual Studio .NET projects without any difficulty. The compiler and linker used in Visual Studio .NET for building Xbox titles are the same as those used in Visual C++ 6.0. As a result, there is no need to change any source code in a Visual C++ 6.0 project for it to build properly under Visual Studio .NET. However, there are several issues you should be aware of.

Enabling Edit and Continue Support

Because Edit and Continue support did not work for Xbox projects in Visual C++ 6.0, Edit and Continue is disabled in Xbox projects created in that environment. Edit and Continue is still disabled in any project you import into Visual Studio .NET. To enable incremental linking and Edit and Continue support in an imported project, do the following:

  1. Make sure the overall Edit and Continue feature is enabled in Visual Studio .NET. By default the feature is enabled, but if you have turned it off for any reason, you need to re-enable it by clicking the Options item on the Tools menu. In the Options dialog box, select the Debugging folder, and then click the Edit and Continue option. Make sure all the check boxes in the Change in Visual C++ Code section are selected, then close the Options dialog by clicking OK.
  2. To enable Edit and Continue in the imported project, select the project's name in the Solution Explorer, and then click Properties on the Project menu. The property pages dialog box appears.
  3. Select Active(Debug) on the Configuration drop-down list.
  4. Select the C/C++ folder, then select the General option.
  5. In the Debug Information Format property, select the Program Database for Edit and Continue (/ZI) option.
  6. Select the Linker folder, and then select the General option.
  7. In the Enable Incremental Linking property, select Yes (/INCREMENTAL).
  8. Close the property pages dialog box by clicking OK.
  9. Click Rebuild Solution on the Build menu.

Note    Xbox projects that you create in Visual Studio .NET already have Edit and Continue support enabled when you create them. You only need to manually enable Edit and Continue in projects that you import from Visual C++ 6.0.

File Names in Custom Build Steps

In Visual Studio .NET, file name arguments in a custom build step (located in the Custom Build Step, General section of the property pages dialog box) must be surrounded by double quotes. Technically, the quotation marks should have been required in Visual C++ 6.0 projects, but Visual C++ 6.0 would let you get away with omitting the double quotes if the file name did not contain any spaces. These quotation marks are mandatory in Visual Studio .NET.

If you import an Xbox project from Visual C++ 6.0 into Visual Studio .NET, the XDK automatically inserts double quotes in the appropriate places. You should be aware of the requirement for quotation marks if you add or modify a custom build step in a newly created Visual Studio .NET project.
